Dilshod Akhmedov
Free
Memory full

You said:

1) Goal Build a Streamlit application that behaves like a mini “data preparation studio”: 1. User uploads a file (CSV/Excel/JSON; plus optional Google Sheets). 2. App profiles the data (types, missingness, duplicates, outliers). 3. User selects cleaning & preparation actions from the UI. 4. App applies transformations to a working copy of the dataset. 5. User dynamically creates visualizations from the transformed data. 6. User exports the final dataset + a report of transformations performed. Your app must support repeatable workflows: the same sequence of operations should be reproducible (via an exported “recipe” or logged steps). 2) Data Inputs (Required) Your app must accept at least: CSV upload (required) Excel upload (.xlsx) (required) JSON upload (required) Google Sheets (via service account or OAuth) (optional) Minimum dataset constraints for testing Your app must handle datasets with: • ≥ 1,000 rows • ≥ 8 columns • Mixed types (numeric + categorical + datetime) • At least some missing values You must include at least 2 sample datasets in your repo for demonstration. I need to build such application explain it to me like a 5 year old

ChatGPT said:

Alright, let’s make this super simple, like you’re 5 👶

🧠 Imagine this…

You have a magic toy machine.

You give it a messy box of LEGO pieces 🧱
And it helps you:

  • Clean them 🧼

  • Sort them 🎨

  • Build something cool 🏗️

  • Save your work 💾

That’s exactly what your Streamlit app will do — but with data instead of LEGO.

🧩 Step-by-step (super easy)

1. 📂 You give it data (like a box of LEGO)

User uploads a file:

  • CSV

  • Excel

  • JSON

👉 Like: “Here is my messy data!”

2. 🔍 The app looks at the data

It says:

  • “This column has numbers 🔢”

  • “This one has names 👤”

  • “Oops! Some values are missing 😢”

  • “Some rows are repeated 🔁”

👉 Like checking your LEGO pieces:

  • “These are red”

  • “These are broken”

  • “These are duplicates”

3. 🧼 You clean the data

You choose buttons like:

  • Remove empty values ❌

  • Fill missing values ➕

  • Remove duplicates 🔁

  • Fix wrong data 🛠️

👉 Like:
“Throw broken LEGO away”
“Replace missing pieces”

4. 🔄 The app works on a copy

Important idea:
👉 Your original data is SAFE

The app makes a copy and works on that.

Like:
You don’t destroy your LEGO box — you play with a copy.

5. 📊 You make charts (fun part!)

You can create:

  • Bar charts 📊

  • Line charts 📈

  • Pie charts 🥧

👉 Like building something cool from LEGO

6. 💾 You download your result

You can:

  • Download clean data

  • Download a report (what you changed)

👉 Like saving your LEGO creation

7. 🔁 You can repeat everything (VERY IMPORTANT ⭐)

Your app remembers steps like:

  • “First I removed empty values”

  • “Then I filled missing data”

This is called a recipe 🍳

👉 So next time:
You can do the same thing again automatically

🏗️ What you are ACTUALLY building

Your app has 5 big parts:

  1. Upload Page

    • Upload file

  2. Data Info Page

    • Show columns, missing values, types

  3. Cleaning Page

    • Buttons to fix data

  4. Visualization Page

    • Create charts

  5. Export Page

    • Download data + recipe

🧠 Simple analogy (final)

Real LifeYour App
LEGO boxDataset
Cleaning LEGOData cleaning
Sorting colorsData profiling
Building toyVisualization
InstructionsRecipe (steps)

🚀 One sentence summary

👉 You are building a smart data cleaner + builder tool where users upload messy data, fix it, visualize it, and save both the result and the steps.

If you want, next I can:

  • break this into actual code structure (Streamlit folders & files)

  • or give you a full starter project you can run immediately


You said:

let's start with simple streamlit app that lets users • Upload file / connect to Sheets • Display: o shape (rows, cols) o column names & inferred dtypes o basic summary stats (numeric + categorical) o missing values by column (count + %) o duplicates count always add one box and show number of columns • Must include a “Reset session” button. lets users do this upload either csv json xlsx or connect google sheets what should I do to achieve this and please give code in chunks don't rewrite my code everytime tell me where to put the code so that it works

ChatGPT said:

Good — this is the right way to build it: small pieces step-by-step 👍
I’ll give you clean chunks, and tell you exactly where to put them.

🧱 STEP 0 — Project structure

Create a folder like this:

data_studio/
│── app.py
│── utils/
│     ├── loader.py
│     └── profiler.py

🧱 STEP 1 — Basic Streamlit app (app.py)

👉 Create app.py

Python
import streamlit as st

st.set_page_config(page_title="Data Studio", layout="wide")

st.title("📊 Mini Data Preparation Studio")

# Reset session button
if st.button("🔄 Reset Session"):
    st.session_state.clear()
    st.rerun()

🧱 STEP 2 — File upload UI

👉 ADD this inside app.py (below the title)

Python
uploaded_file = st.file_uploader(
    "Upload your file",
    type=["csv", "xlsx", "json"]
)

🧱 STEP 3 — Loader logic (utils/loader.py)

👉 Create utils/loader.py

Python
import pandas as pd
import json

def load_file(file):
    if file.name.endswith(".csv"):
        return pd.read_csv(file)

    elif file.name.endswith(".xlsx"):
        return pd.read_excel(file)

    elif file.name.endswith(".json"):
        return pd.read_json(file)

    else:
        return None

🧱 STEP 4 — Use loader in app

👉 BACK to app.py, ADD:

Python
from utils.loader import load_file

df = None

if uploaded_file is not None:
    df = load_file(uploaded_file)
    st.session_state["df"] = df

🧱 STEP 5 — Show basic info

👉 ADD this below loading code:

Python
if "df" in st.session_state:
    df = st.session_state["df"]

    st.subheader("📌 Dataset Overview")

    col1, col2 = st.columns(2)

    with col1:
        st.info(f"Rows: {df.shape[0]}")
        st.info(f"Columns: {df.shape[1]}")

    with col2:
        st.write("Column Names:")
        st.write(list(df.columns))

🧱 STEP 6 — Show data types

👉 ADD:

Python
    st.subheader("📊 Data Types")
    dtypes_df = df.dtypes.reset_index()
    dtypes_df.columns = ["Column", "Type"]
    st.dataframe(dtypes_df)

🧱 STEP 7 — Summary stats

👉 ADD:

Python
    st.subheader("📈 Summary Statistics")

    st.write("Numeric Columns")
    st.dataframe(df.describe())

    st.write("Categorical Columns")
    st.dataframe(df.describe(include=["object"]))

🧱 STEP 8 — Missing values

👉 ADD:

Python
    st.subheader("❗ Missing Values")

    missing = df.isnull().sum()
    missing_percent = (missing / len(df)) * 100

    missing_df = missing.reset_index()
    missing_df.columns = ["Column", "Missing Count"]
    missing_df["Missing %"] = missing_percent.values

    st.dataframe(missing_df)

🧱 STEP 9 — Duplicates

👉 ADD:

Python
    st.subheader("🔁 Duplicates")

    duplicates = df.duplicated().sum()
    st.warning(f"Duplicate rows: {duplicates}")

🧱 STEP 10 — Google Sheets (simple version)

⚠️ Keep it simple first (no OAuth yet)

👉 ADD in app.py ABOVE uploader:

Python
st.subheader("🔗 Google Sheets")

sheet_url = st.text_input("Paste Google Sheets URL")

👉 ADD below:

Python
import pandas as pd

def load_google_sheet(url):
    try:
        csv_url = url.replace("/edit#gid=", "/export?format=csv&gid=")
        return pd.read_csv(csv_url)
    except:
        return None

👉 ADD:

Python
if sheet_url:
    df = load_google_sheet(sheet_url)
    if df is not None:
        st.session_state["df"] = df
    else:
        st.error("Failed to load Google Sheet")

✅ What you have now

Your app can:
✔ Upload CSV / Excel / JSON
✔ Connect to Google Sheets
✔ Show:

  • rows & columns

  • column names

  • data types

  • summary stats

  • missing values

  • duplicates
    ✔ Reset session

🚀 NEXT STEP (tell me when ready)

We can add:

  • per-column cleaning actions (drop/fill)

  • preview before/after

  • transformation history (recipe)

If something breaks, paste your code — I’ll debug it with you.


You said:

nooo all the code should be in just app.py file

ChatGPT said:

Got it 😄 — single file only (app.py), clean and simple.
I’ll still give you small chunks, and tell you exactly where to paste.

🧱 STEP 1 — Basic app + reset button

👉 Create app.py and put this first:

Python
import streamlit as st
import pandas as pd

st.set_page_config(page_title="Data Studio", layout="wide")

st.title("📊 Mini Data Preparation Studio")

# Reset session
if st.button("🔄 Reset Session"):
    st.session_state.clear()
    st.rerun()

🧱 STEP 2 — File upload + Google Sheets input

👉 ADD this below the title section

Python
st.subheader("📂 Load Data")

uploaded_file = st.file_uploader(
    "Upload CSV / Excel / JSON",
    type=["csv", "xlsx", "json"]
)

st.subheader("🔗 Or connect Google Sheets")
sheet_url = st.text_input("Paste Google Sheets URL")

🧱 STEP 3 — Data loading functions

👉 ADD this above everything OR right below imports

Python
def load_file(file):
    if file.name.endswith(".csv"):
        return pd.read_csv(file)
    elif file.name.endswith(".xlsx"):
        return pd.read_excel(file)
    elif file.name.endswith(".json"):
        return pd.read_json(file)
    return None


def load_google_sheet(url):
    try:
        csv_url = url.replace("/edit#gid=", "/export?format=csv&gid=")
        return pd.read_csv(csv_url)
    except:
        return None

🧱 STEP 4 — Load into session

👉 ADD this below upload inputs

Python
if uploaded_file is not None:
    df = load_file(uploaded_file)
    if df is not None:
        st.session_state["df"] = df

if sheet_url:
    df = load_google_sheet(sheet_url)
    if df is not None:
        st.session_state["df"] = df
    else:
        st.error("Failed to load Google Sheet")

🧱 STEP 5 — Display dataset info

👉 ADD this below loading logic

Python
if "df" in st.session_state:
    df = st.session_state["df"]

    st.subheader("📌 Dataset Overview")

    col1, col2, col3 = st.columns(3)

    with col1:
        st.info(f"Rows: {df.shape[0]}")

    with col2:
        st.info(f"Columns: {df.shape[1]}")

    # REQUIRED: separate box for number of columns
    with col3:
        st.success(f"📦 Total Columns: {df.shape[1]}")

    st.write("### Column Names")
    st.write(list(df.columns))

🧱 STEP 6 — Data types

👉 ADD this below overview

Python
    st.subheader("📊 Data Types")

    dtypes_df = df.dtypes.reset_index()
    dtypes_df.columns = ["Column", "Type"]

    st.dataframe(dtypes_df, use_container_width=True)

🧱 STEP 7 — Summary statistics

👉 ADD:

Python
    st.subheader("📈 Summary Statistics")

    st.write("#### Numeric Columns")
    st.dataframe(df.describe(), use_container_width=True)

    st.write("#### Categorical Columns")
    st.dataframe(df.describe(include=["object"]), use_container_width=True)

🧱 STEP 8 — Missing values

👉 ADD:

Python
    st.subheader("❗ Missing Values")

    missing = df.isnull().sum()
    missing_percent = (missing / len(df)) * 100

    missing_df = pd.DataFrame({
        "Column": df.columns,
        "Missing Count": missing.values,
        "Missing %": missing_percent.values
    })

    st.dataframe(missing_df, use_container_width=True)

🧱 STEP 9 — Duplicates

👉 ADD:

Python
    st.subheader("🔁 Duplicates")

    duplicates = df.duplicated().sum()
    st.warning(f"Duplicate rows: {duplicates}")

✅ DONE (what you have now)

Your app can:
✔ Upload CSV / Excel / JSON
✔ Load Google Sheets
✔ Show:

  • rows

  • columns

  • extra column box (requirement ✅)

  • column names

  • data types

  • summary stats

  • missing values (count + %)

  • duplicates
    ✔ Reset session

⚠️ Small improvement (optional but smart)

Wrap describe() to avoid crashes:

Python
if not df.select_dtypes(include="number").empty:
    st.dataframe(df.describe())

🚀 Next step

When you're ready, we’ll add:
👉 data cleaning buttons (drop / fill / replace)
👉 before vs after preview (important for your coursework)
👉 transformation history (recipe system)

Just say: next step 👍


You said:

Page A — Upload & Overview • Upload file / connect to Sheets • Display: o shape (rows, cols) o column names & inferred dtypes o basic summary stats (numeric + categorical) o missing values by column (count + %) o duplicates count always add one box and show number of columns • Must include a “Reset session” button. Page B — Cleaning & Preparation Studio This is the core. Provide UI controls for the operations below, and apply them to the dataset. Page C — Visualization Builder Let the user build charts dynamically from chosen columns, plot type, and optional grouping/aggregation. Page D — Export & Report • Export cleaned dataset (CSV + optionally Excel) • Export a transformation report: o list of steps o parameters used o timestamp • Export either: o a JSON “recipe” (recommended), or o a Python script snippet that replays the pipeline (stretch) now let's build a navigation system to put these pages

ChatGPT said:

Perfect 🔥 now we turn your app into a real multi-page tool — but still inside ONE app.py file.

We’ll use a sidebar navigation (radio buttons) — simple and works perfectly in Streamlit.

🧱 STEP 1 — Create navigation menu

👉 ADD this right after your title (top of file, after reset button)

Python
st.sidebar.title("📂 Navigation")

page = st.sidebar.radio(
    "Go to",
    [
        "Upload & Overview",
        "Cleaning Studio",
        "Visualization Builder",
        "Export & Report"
    ]
)

🧱 STEP 2 — Wrap your current code into Page A

Right now your code shows everything always.

👉 You need to wrap it like this:

🔁 FIND this line in your code:

Python
if "df" in st.session_state:

❗ CHANGE it to:

Python
if page == "Upload & Overview":

    st.header("📂 Upload & Overview")

    # (your upload UI stays here)

    if "df" in st.session_state:
        df = st.session_state["df"]

        # (ALL your current overview code stays here)

👉 So basically:

  • Everything you already built = goes inside Page A

🧱 STEP 3 — Create empty pages (B, C, D)

👉 ADD this AFTER your Page A block

Python
elif page == "Cleaning Studio":
    st.header("🧼 Cleaning & Preparation Studio")

    if "df" not in st.session_state:
        st.warning("Please upload data first")
    else:
        df = st.session_state["df"]
        st.write("Cleaning tools will go here 👇")

👉 ADD next:

Python
elif page == "Visualization Builder":
    st.header("📊 Visualization Builder")

    if "df" not in st.session_state:
        st.warning("Please upload data first")
    else:
        df = st.session_state["df"]
        st.write("Chart builder will go here 👇")

👉 ADD next:

Python
elif page == "Export & Report":
    st.header("💾 Export & Report")

    if "df" not in st.session_state:
        st.warning("Please upload data first")
    else:
        df = st.session_state["df"]
        st.write("Export options will go here 👇")

🧠 What just happened

You now have 4 pages inside 1 file:

PageWhat it does
Upload & Overviewyour current work
Cleaning Studio(next step)
Visualization(charts later)
Export(download later)

⚠️ IMPORTANT RULE

👉 Always use:

Python
st.session_state["df"]

Because:

  • It keeps data when switching pages

  • Without it → data disappears

🧪 How to test

  1. Run app

  2. Upload dataset

  3. Switch pages

  4. Come back → data still there ✅

🚀 Next step (VERY IMPORTANT)

We now build:
👉 Cleaning Studio (real core of your coursework)

  • Drop missing

  • Fill missing

  • Drop columns by %

  • Preview before/after

Just say: “build cleaning studio” and I’ll guide you step-by-step (same style, no rewrites).


You said:

is there any other way to make a navigation buttons a bit more professional like on websites

ChatGPT said:

Yes — good thinking 💯
The sidebar radio works, but it doesn’t look like a real app.

Here are 3 better, more “professional” navigation styles you can use in Streamlit 👇

🥇 OPTION 1 — Top Navigation Bar (Best for you)

This looks like a real website navbar.

👉 Use st.tabs() (clean, built-in, no extra libs)

🧱 Replace your sidebar navigation with this:

👉 REMOVE:

Python
st.sidebar.radio(...)

👉 ADD this below your title

Python
tabs = st.tabs([
    "📂 Upload & Overview",
    "🧼 Cleaning Studio",
    "📊 Visualization Builder",
    "💾 Export & Report"
])

🧱 Wrap your pages like this:

👉 Replace your page logic with:

Python
# TAB 1
with tabs[0]:
    st.header("📂 Upload & Overview")

    # your upload + overview code here


# TAB 2
with tabs[1]:
    st.header("🧼 Cleaning Studio")

    if "df" not in st.session_state:
        st.warning("Upload data first")
    else:
        st.write("Cleaning tools here")


# TAB 3
with tabs[2]:
    st.header("📊 Visualization Builder")

    if "df" not in st.session_state:
        st.warning("Upload data first")
    else:
        st.write("Charts here")


# TAB 4
with tabs[3]:
    st.header("💾 Export & Report")

    if "df" not in st.session_state:
        st.warning("Upload data first")
    else:
        st.write("Export here")

✅ Why this is better

✔ Looks like a real SaaS tool
✔ Clean horizontal navigation
✔ No reruns when switching tabs
✔ Easy to manage

🥈 OPTION 2 — Button Navigation (More control)

Looks like clickable menu buttons.

Python
col1, col2, col3, col4 = st.columns(4)

if col1.button("Upload"):
    st.session_state.page = "upload"

if col2.button("Cleaning"):
    st.session_state.page = "clean"

if col3.button("Visualize"):
    st.session_state.page = "viz"

if col4.button("Export"):
    st.session_state.page = "export"

👉 Then control pages using:

Python
page = st.session_state.get("page", "upload")

⚠️ Downsides

  • More code

  • Slightly less smooth UX

🥉 OPTION 3 — Real Navbar (Advanced, best look)

Use a package like:

👉 streamlit-option-menu

Bash
pip install streamlit-option-menu
Python
from streamlit_option_menu import option_menu

selected = option_menu(
    menu_title=None,
    options=["Upload", "Cleaning", "Visualization", "Export"],
    orientation="horizontal",
)

🔥 This gives you:

  • Real navbar UI

  • Icons

  • Active tab highlight

  • Looks like production app

🧠 My recommendation (for coursework)

👉 Use TABS (st.tabs)

Because:

  • Clean

  • Built-in

  • No dependency issues

  • Looks professional enough for grading

🚀 Next step

Now that navigation is clean, we build:

👉 Cleaning Studio UI (REAL core part)
(with dropdowns, actions, preview)

Just say:
“build cleaning UI” and we continue 💪


You said:

let's make a sidebar now for now put the reset button into the sidebar

ChatGPT said:

Nice 👍 that’s a clean move — sidebar = control panel.

We’ll move Reset button into the sidebar and keep things structured.

🧱 STEP 1 — Create sidebar section

👉 ADD this near the top of your file (after st.title)

Python
st.sidebar.title("⚙️ Controls")

🧱 STEP 2 — Move Reset button into sidebar

👉 REMOVE your old reset button:

Python
if st.button("🔄 Reset Session"):

👉 ADD this instead inside sidebar:

Python
if st.sidebar.button("🔄 Reset Session"):
    st.session_state.clear()
    st.rerun()

🧱 STEP 3 — (Optional but better UX)

Add a divider + info:

Python
st.sidebar.markdown("---")
st.sidebar.write("Use this panel to control the app.")

🧠 Result

Now your app has:

  • Main area → pages/content

  • Sidebar → controls (clean separation)

👉 This is exactly how real dashboards are structured.

🔥 Small pro tip

Later you can also move things like:

  • file upload

  • cleaning options

  • filters

👉 into the sidebar to make UI even cleaner.

Now we can:
